title: SEGMENT 7 — Retinal organoids and the Sasai story

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Retinal organoids and the Sasai story
The retina is one of the most beautiful organoid systems to look at under the microscope, and the history of how it came to exist is one of the more poignant in this field.
Let me tell you about Yoshiki Sasai.
Sasai was a developmental biologist at the RIKEN institute in Kobe, Japan.
He was, by all accounts, an extraordinary scientist — meticulous, creative, deeply intuitive about how embryonic tissues organize themselves.

He had spent most of his career working on the development of the central nervous system in vertebrates, with a particular interest in how the brain and eye form from sheets of neuroectodermal cells in the early embryo.
He understood, better than perhaps anyone of his generation, the signaling logic of how an eye gets built.
In 2011, Sasai's group published a paper in Nature that has to be on any short list of the most striking organoid papers ever produced.
The title was "Self-organizing optic-cup morphogenesis in three-dimensional culture.
" What they had done was take mouse embryonic stem cells, aggregate them into small floating clumps in a specific cocktail of factors, and watch what happened over the following weeks.

What happened was that the cell aggregates spontaneously evaginated — bulged outward — to form vesicles that resembled the optic vesicles that, in a real embryo, evaginate from the neural tube during week three or four of development.
Then those vesicles invaginated — folded back inward — to form cup-shaped structures that resembled the optic cups of a real eye.

Within the cups, the cells differentiated into a layered structure: outer pigmented epithelium on the outside, neural retina on the inside, with photoreceptor precursors at the outermost layer of the neural retina (which corresponds, anatomically, to the deepest layer of the in-vivo retina).
The whole structure was built without any external scaffold, without any guiding mesh, without any sculpting.
The cells did it themselves.
To grasp how astonishing this is, you need to understand what's happening in a normal embryo when an eye forms.
Optic cup morphogenesis is one of the more complex morphogenetic events in development.
It requires precise temporal and spatial coordination of cell migration, proliferation, shape change, and tissue folding.
It involves mechanical forces, signaling gradients, geometric constraints — the whole repertoire of developmental physics.
To recapitulate it in a dish, with just stem cells and a defined medium, was almost magical.

Sasai's group had shown that the entire morphogenetic program for building an eye was, in some sense, encoded in the cells themselves, and given the right initial conditions, the cells would execute that program without any external guidance.
A year later, in 2012, Sasai extended this work to human pluripotent stem cells.
Same general approach, slightly modified protocol.

The result: human retinal organoids — three-dimensional cups of human retinal tissue, with proper lamination, with photoreceptor cells (both rods and cones) appearing in the right layer at roughly the right developmental timing.
You could look at these things under the microscope and see structures that you could only otherwise see in a human fetal eye, which is to say, structures most biologists never get to see at all.
These retinal organoids have become a real workhorse in vision research.

They've been used to model inherited retinal degenerations — diseases like retinitis pigmentosa and Leber congenital amaurosis — by deriving organoids from patient iPSCs and watching the photoreceptors die in the dish, mimicking what happens in the patient's actual retina over years.
They've been used to test gene therapy approaches.
They've been used to study the development of photoreceptor subtypes and to ask basic questions about how cone diversity is specified.
They've even been used in transplant experiments, where small pieces of organoid retinal tissue have been grafted into the eyes of degeneration-model mice and shown to integrate and form connections.

The retinal organoid story is also notable because it was, in some ways, the first really clean demonstration that structural self-organization — not just cell-type specification, but actual tissue morphogenesis with shape changes and folding — could happen in vitro.
Before Sasai's optic cup paper, most people thought you'd need external mechanical inputs, or scaffolds, or some kind of guided assembly, to get organ-like shapes.
Sasai showed you didn't.
Cells, given the right signals and the right boundary conditions, build the shapes themselves.
That insight was foundational.
I want to acknowledge the tragic personal coda.
In 2014, Yoshiki Sasai was caught up in a scandal involving a paper from a colleague's group on a phenomenon called STAP cells, which turned out to be fraudulent.
Sasai was not directly involved in the falsified data, but he had been the senior co-author on the paper and the institutional fallout was severe.
He took his own life in August of that year, at age 52.
The field lost one of its great minds, and the story of organoid research carries that loss with it.
The retinal organoid work, his most enduring contribution, continues to influence the field every day.
